feedback.text: In todays digital world, censorship and monopolistic control pose serious threats to freedom, privacy, and access to information. These threats increasingly come not from governments, but from private companies that act as gatekeepers, deciding what people can see, share, or even pay for online. One major concern is how infrastructure providers and platforms can block access without any legal process. For example, Cloudflare, a major content delivery network, has restricted access to websites like Gelbooru and Danbooru for users who browse through VPNs. This punishes people who value privacy and limits the open nature of the internet. Financial services also play a growing role in censorship. Companies like Visa, MasterCard, and PayPal have reportedly denied service to platforms such as WikiLeaks, OnlyFans, DLsite, and Fantia. When access to income or publishing is controlled by payment processors, it becomes a form of financial censorship that can silence independent creators and alternative voices. Privacy concerns go hand in hand with these issues. Many websites force users to accept cookies or share personal data just to use basic functions. Some services even require uploading a government ID. This level of data collection creates serious risks, especially when private companies store sensitive information. Data breaches, leaks, or even unauthorized surveillance can lead to identity theft or long-term harm. Reliance on cloud services adds to the problem. Increasingly, users need an internet connection just to use products theyve purchased; whether games, software, or even household appliances like fridges and washing machines. When companies control the servers that keep these tools running, ownership becomes meaningless. Access can be revoked at any time, turning users into renters rather than owners. These problems stem from a central issue: the concentration of power. When a few large companies control infrastructure, payments, data, and moderation, they gain the ability to silence, surveil, or exclude users without oversight. This undermines user rights and weakens the foundations of a free, open internet. To protect digital freedom, stronger safeguards are needed. Platforms should not be allowed to block users without clear legal grounds. Personal data should be stored securely, with local or public oversight when possible. And users must be able to access basic digital services without giving up their privacy or independence. Censorship and surveillance no longer require authoritarian governments. Increasingly, they happen through vague policies, technical barriers, and monopolistic practices. To preserve our rights online, we must push for laws that limit corporate control and prioritize the interests of users over profits.
